# Interactions {#sec-ch5}

{{< include ../_common.qmd >}}


## Introduction {#sec-ch5-intro}

In every regression model we have considered so far --- simple linear regression (@sec-ch1), ANCOVA (@sec-ch2), one-way ANOVA (@sec-ch3), and multiple regression with continuous predictors (@sec-ch4) --- we have made a quiet but powerful assumption: the effect of one predictor on the outcome is the **same** regardless of the values of other predictors in the model. Mathematically, the models have been *additive*: each predictor contributes its own piece to the predicted value, and these pieces simply add up.

But in education research, effects rarely operate in isolation. Consider a few questions that additive models cannot answer:

- Does the effect of prior achievement on exam performance **differ between schools**? Perhaps one school is particularly effective at accelerating the progress of high-achieving students, while another provides more support for students entering with lower scores.
- Does the gender pay gap among faculty **vary across academic ranks**? The gap might be negligible for assistant professors but widen substantially at the full professor level.
- Does the relationship between education and crime rates **depend on urbanization**? Education might be protective against crime in rural areas but less so in highly urbanized environments.

When the effect of one variable depends on the value of another, we say there is an **interaction** between the two variables. This phenomenon is also called **effect modification** --- one variable *modifies* the effect of the other. The variables involved in an interaction are sometimes called **moderators**: the effect of $X$ on $Y$ is *moderated by* $Z$.

Interactions represent a fundamentally different way of thinking about predictors. Rather than asking "what is the effect of $X$?", we ask "what is the effect of $X$, **and for whom** or **under what conditions**?" This shift from a single-number summary to a context-dependent effect is one of the most important conceptual transitions in applied regression analysis.

This chapter covers three types of interactions, organized by the nature of the variables involved:

1. **Continuous $\times$ Categorical** (@sec-ch5-cont-cat): Does the slope of a continuous predictor differ across groups? This is the most common and most intuitive type of interaction, and we develop it in the greatest detail.
2. **Categorical $\times$ Categorical** (@sec-ch5-cat-cat): Does a group difference depend on the level of another categorical factor?
3. **Continuous $\times$ Continuous** (@sec-ch5-cont-cont): Does the effect of one continuous variable change as another continuous variable increases?

::: {.objectives}
[Learning Objectives]{.obj-title}

After completing this chapter, you will be able to:

1. Explain why additive models are sometimes insufficient and articulate the concept of effect modification
2. Specify and interpret interaction terms in regression models for all three types of interactions
3. Derive separate group-specific regression equations from a single interaction model
4. Explain how adding an interaction changes the interpretation of **all** coefficients in the model
5. Conduct formal tests of interactions using nested model comparisons
6. Probe significant interactions using conditional slopes and conditional comparisons with the `marginaleffects` package
7. Distinguish between Type II and Type III sums of squares for testing main effects in the presence of interactions
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## A Note on the Worked Examples: Test First, Then Interpret
This chapter uses three real education datasets --- school exam scores, faculty salaries, and county crime rates --- to illustrate how interactions are *specified, interpreted, tested, and probed*. A candid feature of these examples is worth stating up front: **in all three, the formal test of the interaction comes back non-significant**:

- Continuous $\times$ Categorical (LRT $\times$ school): $F(1, 126) = 2.36$, $p = 0.13$
- Categorical $\times$ Categorical (gender $\times$ rank): $F(2, 508) = 0.901$, $p = 0.41$
- Continuous $\times$ Continuous (education $\times$ urbanization): interaction $t \approx -0.97$, $p = 0.34$

This is not a defect of the examples --- it is realistic. Interactions are *higher-order* effects that are notoriously hard to detect in noisy observational education data, and a non-significant result is the most common outcome in practice. We therefore use these examples to teach the **mechanics of interpreting and testing** an interaction --- how to read each coefficient, derive group-specific equations, visualize conditional effects, and run the formal test --- while showing what we *would* conclude if the test were significant. The overarching lesson is simple: **always test an interaction before interpreting it as real**, and remember that a statistically significant interaction (effects *differ*) is not the same as an important one (the difference is *large enough to matter*).
:::


## Continuous by Categorical Interaction {#sec-ch5-cont-cat}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Connection to Chapter 2
In @sec-ch2 (ANCOVA), we compared **Battersea (school 15) and Chelsea (school 6)** while adjusting for prior achievement (LRT), and estimated an adjusted Chelsea advantage of about **7.51 GCSE points**. A critical assumption of that model was **parallel regression lines** --- the LRT slope was forced to be identical across schools, and the only difference was a constant vertical shift in intercepts. In this section we **release that constraint** by adding an interaction term, allowing each school to have its own slope. To make the slope difference easy to see, we examine a **different pair of schools here --- Chelsea (school 6) and Hackney (school 10)** --- and let the data tell us whether the LRT-GCSE relationship truly differs between schools.
:::

The continuous by categorical interaction is the most natural entry point into the topic of interactions because it connects directly to a model we already know well: the ANCOVA model from @sec-ch2. Recall that in @sec-ch2 we compared two London schools --- **Chelsea and Battersea** --- with parallel slopes, adjusting for prior achievement (LRT scores). The key assumption of that model was that the two schools had **parallel regression lines**: the same LRT slope, differing only in their intercepts. Here we relax that assumption, but to make the slope difference visible we switch to a **different pair, Chelsea and Hackney**.

But is that assumption justified? Look at the raw data from these two schools. Some schools may show more progress for high-achieving students while others show more progress for low-achieving students. Chelsea school has a higher mean GCSE score than Hackney school after adjusting for LRT, and the difference appears particularly pronounced for students with high LRT scores. If the estimated slope for Chelsea is about 0.54 and for Hackney is about 0.30, the lines look noticeably different in steepness. This suggests that the *effect of prior achievement on exam performance differs between schools* --- an interaction.

### From ANCOVA to the Interaction Model

Let us build up the interaction model step by step, starting from the ANCOVA model we already understand.

**Step 1: The ANCOVA Model (Parallel Lines)**

Define a dummy variable for Chelsea school:

$$
d_{Ci} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if student } i \text{ is in Chelsea school} \\ 0 & \text{if student } i \text{ is in Hackney school} \end{cases}
$$

The standard ANCOVA model is:

$$
y_i = \alpha + \beta_1 x_i + \beta_2 d_{Ci} + \epsilon_i
$$ {#eq-ch5-ancova}

An important assumption of this model is that both schools share the **same slope** $\beta_1$ --- the regression lines are parallel. The only difference between schools is captured by $\beta_2$, a constant vertical shift that applies equally at every value of LRT.

**Step 2: Relaxing the Parallel Lines Assumption**

To allow the LRT slope to differ between schools, we add a **product term** $x_i \times d_{Ci}$ --- the interaction between LRT score and the school dummy:

$$
y_i = \alpha + \beta_1 x_i + \beta_2 d_{Ci} + \beta_3 (x_i \times d_{Ci}) + \epsilon_i
$$ {#eq-ch5-contcat}

This single equation actually encodes two *different* regression lines, one for each school. To see this, we can rewrite @eq-ch5-contcat by collecting terms:

$$
y_i = \underbrace{(\alpha + \beta_2 d_{Ci})}_{\text{intercept function}} + \underbrace{(\beta_1 + \beta_3 d_{Ci})}_{\text{slope function}} \cdot x_i + \epsilon_i
$$ {#eq-ch5-contcat-grouped}

Both the intercept and the slope are now **functions of the school dummy** $d_{Ci}$. This is the essence of an interaction: the relationship between $x$ and $y$ (the slope) depends on which group a student belongs to.

### Deriving Group-Specific Equations

By substituting $d_{Ci} = 0$ and $d_{Ci} = 1$ into @eq-ch5-contcat-grouped, we obtain the regression equation for each school:

**For Hackney school** ($d_{Ci} = 0$):

$$
\text{E}(y_i \mid d_{Ci} = 0, x_i) = \alpha + \beta_1 x_i
$$ {#eq-ch5-hackney}

**For Chelsea school** ($d_{Ci} = 1$):

$$
\text{E}(y_i \mid d_{Ci} = 1, x_i) = (\alpha + \beta_2) + (\beta_1 + \beta_3) x_i
$$ {#eq-ch5-chelsea}

Notice that Chelsea has a **different intercept** ($\alpha + \beta_2$) *and* a **different slope** ($\beta_1 + \beta_3$) from Hackney. One model with four parameters encodes two completely separate regression lines.

### The School Difference Depends on LRT

A crucial consequence of the interaction is that the difference between schools is no longer a single number. Subtracting @eq-ch5-hackney from @eq-ch5-chelsea:

$$
\underbrace{\text{E}(y_i \mid d_{Ci} = 1, x_i)}_{\text{Chelsea}} - \underbrace{\text{E}(y_i \mid d_{Ci} = 0, x_i)}_{\text{Hackney}} = \beta_2 + \beta_3 x_i
$$ {#eq-ch5-school-diff}

The school difference is $\beta_2 + \beta_3 x_i$ --- it **depends on the LRT score** $x_i$. For students with low LRT scores, the difference is close to $\beta_2$ (the intercept difference). As LRT increases, the difference changes by $\beta_3$ for each additional unit. With estimated values of $b_2 = 9.28$ and $b_3 = 0.24$, the point estimates suggest Chelsea's advantage over Hackney *grows* as students' prior achievement increases. Note, however, that $b_3 = 0.24$ is only *suggestive*: as the formal F-test in @sec-ch5-testing shows, the slope difference is **not statistically significant** ($F(1, 126) = 2.36$, $p = 0.13$). We use this example to practice *interpreting* an interaction's coefficients, not as established evidence that the slopes differ.

### Interpretation of Each Coefficient {#sec-ch5-coef-interpret}

Each coefficient in the interaction model has a precise interpretation:

- $\alpha$: Mean GCSE score for Hackney school when LRT equals zero (intercept for the reference group)
- $\beta_1$: Increase in mean GCSE score per unit increase in LRT, **for Hackney school** (slope for the reference group)
- $\beta_2$: Difference in mean GCSE between Chelsea and Hackney **when LRT equals zero** (intercept difference)
- $\beta_3$ has **two equivalent interpretations** --- this is a hallmark of interactions:
    1. Difference in the LRT slope between Chelsea and Hackney (the effect of LRT *depends on* school)
    2. Increase in the Chelsea--Hackney difference per unit increase in LRT (the effect of school *depends on* LRT)

::: {.callout-note}
## The Symmetry of Interactions
The interaction coefficient $\beta_3$ can be read in two ways because the product $x_i \times d_{Ci}$ is symmetric. We can rewrite @eq-ch5-contcat as:

$$
y_i = \underbrace{(\alpha + \beta_1 x_i)}_{\text{Hackney line}} + \underbrace{(\beta_2 + \beta_3 x_i)}_{\text{Chelsea} - \text{Hackney difference}} \cdot d_{Ci} + \epsilon_i
$$

Reading across: the effect of LRT depends on school. Reading down: the effect of school depends on LRT. Both readings describe the same phenomenon --- the non-additivity of the two predictors.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Effect Modifiers and Moderators
Variables involved in interactions are sometimes called **effect modifiers** or **moderators**. In our example, school *modifies* the effect of LRT on GCSE (or equivalently, LRT *moderates* the school difference). The terminology is symmetric: either variable can be called the moderator, depending on the research question being asked.
:::

### Fitting the ANCOVA and Interaction Models

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: ch5-cont-cat-setup
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Setup: select two schools from the GCSE data
# (Chelsea = school 6, Hackney = school 10; Hackney is the reference)
two_schools <- gcse |>
  filter(school %in% c(6, 10)) |>
  mutate(school_name = factor(
    ifelse(school == 10, "Hackney", "Chelsea"),
    levels = c("Hackney", "Chelsea")
  ))

# ANCOVA model: parallel lines (same slope, different intercepts)
model_parallel <- lm(gcse ~ lrt + school_name, data = two_schools)

# Interaction model: non-parallel lines (different slopes AND intercepts)
model_interact <- lm(gcse ~ lrt * school_name, data = two_schools)
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cont-cat-setup"
#| echo: false
```
- This code chunk produces no printed output --- it creates the data subset (`two_schools`) and fits both models (`model_parallel` and `model_interact`) for use in subsequent analyses
- The `two_schools` data frame contains students from schools 6 and 10, with a factor variable `school_name` indicating Hackney (reference) or Chelsea
- Both model objects are stored and will be examined in the next panel-tabset

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cont-cat-setup"
```
- We subset the `gcse` data to include only schools 6 (Chelsea) and 10 (Hackney), creating a two-group comparison
- `school_name` is created as a factor with Hackney as the reference level (listed first in `levels`), so the dummy variable $d_{Ci}$ equals 1 for Chelsea
- `model_parallel` fits the ANCOVA from @eq-ch5-ancova: `gcse ~ lrt + school_name` specifies parallel lines
- `model_interact` fits the interaction model from @eq-ch5-contcat: `lrt * school_name` is R shorthand for `lrt + school_name + lrt:school_name`


:::

### Comparing Model Output

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: ch5-cont-cat-compare-parallel
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# ANCOVA Model (Parallel Lines)
model_parameters(model_parallel) |> print_md()

# Interaction Model (Non-Parallel Lines)
model_parameters(model_interact) |> print_md()
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cont-cat-compare-parallel"
#| echo: false
```
**ANCOVA Model** (3 parameters):

- `(Intercept)`: Mean GCSE for Hackney when LRT = 0
- `lrt`: Common LRT slope for both schools
- `school_nameChelsea`: Adjusted mean difference (Chelsea $-$ Hackney), assumed constant across all LRT values

**Interaction Model** (4 parameters):

- `(Intercept)` ($\alpha$): Mean GCSE for Hackney when LRT = 0
- `lrt` ($\beta_1$): LRT slope **for Hackney only**
- `school_nameChelsea` ($\beta_2$): School difference **only when LRT = 0** (estimated $b_2 \approx 9.28$)
- `lrt:school_nameChelsea` ($\beta_3$): **Difference in slopes** between Chelsea and Hackney (estimated $b_3 \approx 0.24$, not significant; see @sec-ch5-testing)

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cont-cat-compare-parallel"
```
- `model_parameters()` from the `easystats` ecosystem provides a clean summary of each model's coefficients
- In the ANCOVA model, the `school_nameChelsea` coefficient represents the school difference at *all* LRT values (constant shift)
- In the interaction model, `school_nameChelsea` is the school difference *only when LRT = 0*, and `lrt:school_nameChelsea` captures how that difference changes with LRT


:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Adding an Interaction Changes the Meaning of ALL Coefficients!
This is one of the most common sources of confusion in regression analysis. Compare the interpretation of $\beta_2$ (the school coefficient) across the two models:

| | ANCOVA (no interaction) | Interaction model |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **$\beta_2$ means** | Adjusted mean difference for **all** LRT values | Difference **only when LRT = 0** |
| **$\beta_1$ means** | LRT slope for **both** schools | LRT slope for **Hackney only** |
| **School difference** | Constant: $\beta_2$ | Varies: $\beta_2 + \beta_3 x_i$ |

When you add an interaction, the so-called "main effects" become **conditional effects** --- they describe the effect of one variable *at a specific value* (typically zero) of the other variable. The coefficients that looked the same in the output have fundamentally different interpretations.
:::

### Annotated Coefficient Diagram

The following diagram illustrates each coefficient in the interaction model geometrically. The two regression lines are drawn with annotations showing what each parameter controls.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: fig-ch5-coef-diagram
#| fig-cap: "Annotated coefficient diagram for the continuous x categorical interaction model. Each coefficient is labeled with its geometric role: alpha is the Hackney intercept, beta_1 is the Hackney slope, beta_2 is the intercept gap at x = 0, and beta_3 is the slope difference."
#| fig-alt: "Annotated line plot with LRT score on the x-axis and GCSE score on the y-axis showing two non-parallel regression lines for Hackney and Chelsea schools. Arrows and labels mark each coefficient: alpha labels the Hackney intercept on the y-axis, beta_1 labels the Hackney line slope, beta_2 shows the vertical intercept gap between the two lines at x equals zero, and beta_3 highlights the difference in slopes between the two school lines."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Extract coefficients from the interaction model
b <- coef(model_interact)
alpha <- b[1]
beta1 <- b[2]
beta2 <- b[3]
beta3 <- b[4]

# Define x range for the lines
x_range <- range(two_schools$lrt)
x_min <- x_range[1]
x_max <- x_range[2]

# A reference x value for illustrating the slope difference
x_ref <- mean(two_schools$lrt)

# Hackney line values
y_hack_0 <- alpha
y_hack_ref <- alpha + beta1 * x_ref

# Chelsea line values
y_chel_0 <- alpha + beta2
y_chel_ref <- (alpha + beta2) + (beta1 + beta3) * x_ref

ggplot() +
  # Hackney regression line

  geom_abline(
    intercept = alpha, slope = beta1,
    color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], linewidth = 1.3
  ) +

  # Chelsea regression line
  geom_abline(
    intercept = alpha + beta2, slope = beta1 + beta3,
    color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], linewidth = 1.3
  ) +

  # (a) alpha: Hackney intercept -- dot at (0, alpha)
  geom_point(aes(x = 0, y = alpha), size = 4,
             color = ber640_colors[["primary"]]) +
  annotate("text", x = -1.5, y = alpha - 2.5, label = expression(alpha ~ "(Hackney intercept)"),
           hjust = 0.5, size = 4, color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], fontface = "bold") +

  # (b) beta1: Hackney slope -- arrow along the Hackney line
  annotate("segment",
           x = x_ref - 5, y = alpha + beta1 * (x_ref - 5),
           xend = x_ref + 5, yend = alpha + beta1 * (x_ref + 5),
           color = ber640_colors[["success"]], linewidth = 1.5,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.25, "cm"), type = "closed")) +
  annotate("text", x = x_ref + 6, y = alpha + beta1 * (x_ref + 5) + 1,
           label = expression(beta[1] ~ "(Hackney slope)"),
           hjust = 0, size = 4, color = ber640_colors[["success"]], fontface = "bold") +

  # (c) beta2: Vertical gap at x = 0
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, y = alpha, xend = 0, yend = alpha + beta2),
               color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], linewidth = 1.2,
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"), ends = "both")) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.5, y = alpha + beta2 / 2,
           label = expression(beta[2] ~ "(intercept gap at x = 0)"),
           hjust = 0, size = 4, color = ber640_colors[["secondary"]], fontface = "bold") +

  # (d) beta3: Slope difference -- show at x_ref
  geom_segment(aes(x = x_ref + 8, y = alpha + beta1 * (x_ref + 8),
                   xend = x_ref + 8, yend = (alpha + beta2) + (beta1 + beta3) * (x_ref + 8)),
               color = ber640_colors[["warning"]], linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed",
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"), ends = "both")) +
  annotate("text", x = x_ref + 9, y = ((alpha + beta1 * (x_ref + 8)) +
                                         ((alpha + beta2) + (beta1 + beta3) * (x_ref + 8))) / 2,
           label = expression(beta[2] + beta[3] %.% x),
           hjust = 0, size = 4, color = ber640_colors[["warning"]], fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = x_ref + 9,
           y = ((alpha + beta1 * (x_ref + 8)) +
                ((alpha + beta2) + (beta1 + beta3) * (x_ref + 8))) / 2 - 2,
           label = expression("(" * beta[3] ~ "= slope difference)"),
           hjust = 0, size = 3.5, color = ber640_colors[["warning"]]) +

  # Labels for lines
  annotate("text", x = x_max - 2, y = alpha + beta1 * (x_max - 2) - 2,
           label = "Hackney", size = 4.5, color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = x_max - 2,
           y = (alpha + beta2) + (beta1 + beta3) * (x_max - 2) + 2,
           label = "Chelsea", size = 4.5, color = ber640_colors[["accent"]], fontface = "bold") +

  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-5, x_max + 15),
                  ylim = c(min(alpha, alpha + beta2) - 5,
                           max(alpha + beta1 * x_max,
                               (alpha + beta2) + (beta1 + beta3) * x_max) + 5)) +
  labs(
    title = "Anatomy of the Interaction Model Coefficients",
    subtitle = expression(y[i] == alpha + beta[1] * x[i] + beta[2] * d[Ci] + beta[3] * (x[i] %*% d[Ci]) + epsilon[i]),
    x = "LRT Score", y = "Predicted GCSE Score"
  )
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-coef-diagram"
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
```
- The diagram makes the four-parameter model concrete: one intercept ($\alpha$), one slope ($\beta_1$), one intercept shift ($\beta_2$), and one slope shift ($\beta_3$) encode two complete regression lines
- The vertical gap between the lines at $x = 0$ is exactly $\beta_2$. As $x$ increases, the gap changes by $\beta_3$ per unit, reflecting the interaction
- If $\beta_3 > 0$, the lines diverge (Chelsea's advantage grows with LRT); if $\beta_3 < 0$, they converge
- This visual reinforces the key insight: in an interaction model, the "main effect" $\beta_2$ is only the group difference at $x = 0$, not a universal group difference

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-coef-diagram"
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
```
- Two regression lines are drawn using `geom_abline()` with the estimated intercepts and slopes from `model_interact`
- **$\alpha$** (navy dot at $x = 0$): The Hackney intercept, shown as a point on the y-axis where the Hackney line crosses $x = 0$
- **$\beta_1$** (teal arrow): The Hackney slope, illustrated as the direction and steepness of the Hackney line
- **$\beta_2$** (rust double-ended arrow at $x = 0$): The vertical gap between the two lines at the y-axis, representing the intercept difference between Chelsea and Hackney
- **$\beta_3$** (gold dashed segment): The slope difference, which causes the lines to diverge. The growing gap between lines at higher LRT values visually shows $\beta_2 + \beta_3 x$
- `coord_cartesian()` extends the plot slightly beyond the data range so the intercept annotations at $x = 0$ are visible


:::

### Interactive Exploration: Parallel vs Non-Parallel Lines {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try It Yourself

This app has a single control: the **Interaction (β₃) slider** — drag it as your first move. Watch the vertical arrows at three probe points (LRT = -10, 0, +10) — they show that the school difference is **not a single number** but depends on where you look along the x-axis. (The intercept α, the Hackney slope β₁, and the intercept gap β₂ are fixed at their fitted values from the real GCSE data, so only β₃ moves.)

- **β₃ = 0**: Lines are parallel — this is the ANCOVA model
- **β₃ > 0**: Chelsea's line is steeper — Chelsea's advantage grows with LRT
- **β₃ < 0**: Chelsea's line is flatter — Chelsea's advantage shrinks (or reverses) with LRT
:::

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: ch5-ojs-interaction-data

# Pass two_schools data (already created above) to OJS
int_schools_ojs <- two_schools |>
  select(lrt, gcse, school_name) |>
  filter(!is.na(lrt), !is.na(gcse))

ojs_define(int_two_schools_r = int_schools_ojs)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

import { ber640 as int_ber640, categorical as int_categorical,
         withAlpha as int_withAlpha,
         themeColors as int_themeColors,
         plotDefaults as int_plotDefaults,
         formatCoef as int_formatCoef, formatSigned as int_formatSigned,
         seq as int_seq,
         srSummary as int_srSummary, resetControls as int_resetControls,
         onThemeChange as int_onThemeChange } from "../ojs/shared.js"
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

int_theme = int_themeColors()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

int_pill = (label, value, tone = "") => html`<span class="ojs-readout ${tone ? "ojs-readout-" + tone : ""}"><span class="ojs-readout-label">${label}</span><span class="ojs-readout-value">${value}</span></span>`
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

int_two_schools = transpose(int_two_schools_r)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

viewof int_beta3 = Inputs.range([-1.5, 1.5], {
  value: 0.24,
  step: 0.01,
  label: html`<strong>Interaction (β₃)</strong>`,
  width: 280
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Fixed coefficients from the actual model (only β₃ is interactive)
int_alpha = -3.25
int_beta1 = 0.30
int_beta2 = 9.28

// Derived school-specific parameters
int_hackney_intercept = int_alpha
int_hackney_slope = int_beta1
int_chelsea_intercept = int_alpha + int_beta2
int_chelsea_slope = int_beta1 + int_beta3

// Probe points
int_probes = [-10, 0, 10]
int_probe_data = int_probes.map(x => {
  const y_hackney = int_hackney_intercept + int_hackney_slope * x;
  const y_chelsea = int_chelsea_intercept + int_chelsea_slope * x;
  const gap = y_chelsea - y_hackney;
  return { x, y_hackney, y_chelsea, gap };
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Line data
int_x_range = {
  const vals = int_two_schools.map(d => d.lrt);
  return [Math.min(...vals) - 1, Math.max(...vals) + 1];
}

int_line_hackney = int_seq(int_x_range[0], int_x_range[1], 100).map(x => ({
  x, y: int_hackney_intercept + int_hackney_slope * x, school: "Hackney"
}))
int_line_chelsea = int_seq(int_x_range[0], int_x_range[1], 100).map(x => ({
  x, y: int_chelsea_intercept + int_chelsea_slope * x, school: "Chelsea"
}))
int_line_points = int_line_hackney.concat(int_line_chelsea)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Status flag (preserved Phase-2 fix: parallel threshold + b3=0.24 significance note)
int_isParallel = Math.abs(int_beta3) < 0.005
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

int_readouts = html`<div class="ojs-readouts">
  ${int_pill("Status", int_isParallel ? "Parallel lines (ANCOVA)" : "Non-parallel lines", int_isParallel ? "good" : "warning")}
  ${int_pill("Δ @ LRT −10", int_formatSigned(int_probe_data[0].gap, 2), "info")}
  ${int_pill("Δ @ LRT 0", int_formatSigned(int_probe_data[1].gap, 2), "info")}
  ${int_pill("Δ @ LRT +10", int_formatSigned(int_probe_data[2].gap, 2), "info")}
</div>`
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Interactive scatter plot
int_plot = Plot.plot(int_plotDefaults({
  width: 680,
  height: 340,
  marginTop: 24,
  marginBottom: 40,
  marginLeft: 48,
  marginRight: 16,
  style: { color: int_theme.text },
  color: {
    domain: ["Hackney", "Chelsea"],
    range: [int_categorical[0], int_categorical[1]],
    legend: true
  },
  x: { label: "LRT Score", domain: int_x_range, grid: true },
  y: { label: "GCSE Score", grid: true },
  marks: [
    // Data points
    Plot.dot(int_two_schools, {
      x: "lrt",
      y: "gcse",
      fill: d => d.school_name,
      opacity: 0.4,
      r: 3
    }),

    // Regression lines (non-color channel: Chelsea dashed vs Hackney solid)
    Plot.line(int_line_points, {
      x: "x",
      y: "y",
      stroke: "school",
      strokeWidth: 2.5,
      strokeDasharray: d => d.school === "Chelsea" ? "8,4" : null
    }),

    // Probe point markers on Hackney line
    Plot.dot(int_probe_data, {
      x: "x", y: "y_hackney",
      fill: int_categorical[0],
      r: 5, stroke: int_theme.bg, strokeWidth: 1
    }),

    // Probe point markers on Chelsea line
    Plot.dot(int_probe_data, {
      x: "x", y: "y_chelsea",
      fill: int_categorical[1],
      r: 5, stroke: int_theme.bg, strokeWidth: 1
    }),

    // Gap arrows at probe points
    Plot.link(int_probe_data, {
      x1: "x", y1: "y_hackney",
      x2: "x", y2: "y_chelsea",
      stroke: int_theme.text,
      strokeWidth: 1.5,
      strokeDasharray: "4,3"
    }),

    // Gap labels
    Plot.text(int_probe_data, {
      x: "x", y: d => (d.y_hackney + d.y_chelsea) / 2,
      text: d => `Δ = ${int_formatCoef(d.gap, 1)}`,
      dx: 28,
      fill: int_theme.text,
      fontSize: 11,
      fontWeight: 600
    })
  ]
}))
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

int_sr = int_srSummary("ch05-parallel",
  `Interaction b3 = ${int_formatCoef(int_beta3, 2)}. Hackney slope ${int_formatCoef(int_hackney_slope, 2)}, Chelsea slope ${int_formatCoef(int_chelsea_slope, 2)}. ` +
  (int_isParallel
    ? "Lines are parallel, matching the ANCOVA model."
    : "Lines look non-parallel — the school difference varies with LRT. ") +
  `Chelsea minus Hackney gap is ${int_formatSigned(int_probe_data[0].gap, 1)} at LRT -10, ${int_formatSigned(int_probe_data[1].gap, 1)} at LRT 0, and ${int_formatSigned(int_probe_data[2].gap, 1)} at LRT +10. At the fitted b3 = 0.24 this slope difference is not statistically significant (see the F-test).`)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

int_app = html`<div class="ojs-app" role="group" aria-label="Parallel vs non-parallel regression lines by school">
  <div class="ojs-controls">${viewof int_beta3}</div>
  <div class="ojs-equation">
    <strong style="color:${int_categorical[0]}">Hackney:</strong>
    ŷ = ${int_formatCoef(int_hackney_intercept, 2)} + ${int_formatCoef(int_hackney_slope, 2)} × LRT
    <span style="margin-left:1.5rem;"><strong style="color:${int_categorical[1]}">Chelsea:</strong>
    ŷ = ${int_formatCoef(int_chelsea_intercept, 2)} + ${int_formatCoef(int_chelsea_slope, 2)} × LRT</span>
  </div>
  ${int_readouts}
  <div class="ojs-plot">${int_plot}</div>
  ${int_sr}
</div>`
```

::: {.callout-note}
## What you should have seen

- At β₃ = 0 the two lines are parallel (the ANCOVA model) and the Chelsea–Hackney gap is identical at every probe point — a single number.
- As soon as β₃ ≠ 0 the lines fan out: the gap (Δ) at LRT −10, 0, and +10 are no longer equal, so the school difference becomes a *function of LRT*, not a constant.
- The sign of β₃ sets the direction: β₃ > 0 means Chelsea's advantage grows with prior achievement, β₃ < 0 means it shrinks or reverses.
:::

### Raw Data: Do the Slopes Look Different?

Before fitting any models, it is always wise to look at the raw data. The scatterplot below shows the actual GCSE scores plotted against LRT scores, colored by school, with separate best-fit lines overlaid.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: fig-ch5-raw-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Raw GCSE scores versus LRT scores by school, with separate regression lines. The lines appear to diverge, suggesting that the LRT-GCSE relationship may differ between schools."
#| fig-alt: "Scatter plot with LRT score on the x-axis and GCSE score on the y-axis. Points are colored by school, Hackney and Chelsea. Two separate regression lines are overlaid, one per school. The lines diverge as LRT increases, with the Chelsea line rising more steeply, suggesting the LRT-GCSE relationship is stronger for Chelsea students than for Hackney students."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

ggplot(two_schools, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse, color = school_name)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, se = TRUE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(ber640_colors[["primary"]], ber640_colors[["secondary"]])) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(ber640_colors[["primary"]], ber640_colors[["secondary"]])) +
  labs(
    title = "GCSE vs LRT by School",
    subtitle = "Do the regression lines look parallel?",
    x = "LRT Score (Prior Achievement)",
    y = "GCSE Score (Exam Performance)",
    color = "School", fill = "School"
  )
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-raw-scatter"
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
```
- Each point represents one student's LRT and GCSE scores, colored by their school
- The two regression lines allow a visual comparison of slopes: if Chelsea's line is steeper than Hackney's, it suggests Chelsea students show a stronger relationship between prior achievement and exam performance
- The degree to which the lines diverge as LRT increases is a visual approximation of the interaction effect
- The overlapping confidence bands at certain LRT values indicate uncertainty about the slope difference in those regions

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-raw-scatter"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
```
- `geom_point(alpha = 0.4)` plots the raw data with transparency to reduce overplotting
- `geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x)` overlays separate OLS regression lines for each school (because `color = school_name` is mapped in the main `aes()`)
- The shaded bands are 95% confidence intervals around each fitted line
- If the lines are parallel (same slope), the ANCOVA model from @sec-ch2 is adequate; if they diverge or converge, an interaction may be needed


:::

### Visualizing the Interaction

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: fig-ch5-cont-cat-parallel-vs-interact
#| fig-cap: "Comparison of the ANCOVA model (parallel lines, left) and the interaction model (non-parallel lines, right). The interaction model allows the LRT slope to differ between schools."
#| fig-alt: "Side-by-side fitted-line plots of predicted GCSE versus LRT, each showing two fitted regression lines with 95 percent confidence bands (one per school) and no raw data points. The left panel displays the ANCOVA model with two parallel regression lines differing only in intercept. The right panel displays the interaction model with two non-parallel regression lines that diverge as LRT increases, allowing the slope to differ between schools. The comparison highlights how the interaction model provides a more flexible fit."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Left panel: ANCOVA (parallel lines)
p1 <- plot_predictions(model_parallel, condition = c("lrt", "school_name")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(ber640_colors[["primary"]], ber640_colors[["secondary"]])) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(ber640_colors[["primary"]], ber640_colors[["secondary"]])) +
  labs(
    title = "ANCOVA: Parallel Lines",
    subtitle = "Same slope, different intercepts",
    x = "LRT Score", y = "Predicted GCSE Score",
    color = "School", fill = "School"
  )

# Right panel: Interaction (non-parallel lines)
p2 <- plot_predictions(model_interact, condition = c("lrt", "school_name")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(ber640_colors[["primary"]], ber640_colors[["secondary"]])) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(ber640_colors[["primary"]], ber640_colors[["secondary"]])) +
  labs(
    title = "Interaction: Non-Parallel Lines",
    subtitle = "Different slopes AND intercepts",
    x = "LRT Score", y = "Predicted GCSE Score",
    color = "School", fill = "School"
  )

p1 + p2 + plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-cont-cat-parallel-vs-interact"
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5
```
- In the left panel (ANCOVA), the distance between the two lines is the same everywhere --- this is the parallel lines assumption
- In the right panel (interaction), the lines spread apart as LRT increases, reflecting the positive (though not statistically significant) slope difference: the point estimates put Chelsea's advantage over Hackney as growing for students with higher prior achievement
- The confidence bands (shaded regions) are widest at the extremes of LRT where data are sparser

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-cont-cat-parallel-vs-interact"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5
```
- `plot_predictions()` from the `marginaleffects` package draws the fitted regression lines with confidence bands
- The left panel shows the ANCOVA model: two parallel lines separated by a constant vertical distance $\beta_2$
- The right panel shows the interaction model: the lines are free to have different slopes, and they diverge (or converge) as LRT increases
- `patchwork` combines the two panels side by side, with `plot_layout(guides = "collect")` sharing the legend


:::

### The School Difference Across LRT Values

Since the school difference is $\beta_2 + \beta_3 x_i$ (@eq-ch5-school-diff), we can visualize how it changes across the range of LRT scores.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: fig-ch5-school-diff-by-lrt
#| fig-cap: "The estimated difference between Chelsea and Hackney schools (with 95% confidence interval) as a function of LRT score. The point estimate of the school difference is not constant --- it increases with prior achievement --- though the slope of this relationship (b3) is not statistically significant."
#| fig-alt: "Line plot with LRT score on the x-axis and the estimated Chelsea minus Hackney difference in GCSE on the y-axis. A solid line shows the point estimate of the school difference, surrounded by a shaded 95 percent confidence band. The difference starts near zero at low LRT values and increases at higher LRT values, suggesting the school gap grows with prior achievement, though the confidence band is wide."
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Compute the school comparison at each LRT value
plot_comparisons(
  model_interact,
  variables = "school_name",
  condition = "lrt"
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title = "School Difference Depends on Prior Achievement",
    subtitle = expression(paste("Estimated difference (Chelsea - Hackney) = ",
                                hat(beta)[2], " + ", hat(beta)[3], " x LRT")),
    x = "LRT Score",
    y = "Predicted GCSE Difference\n(Chelsea - Hackney)"
  )
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-school-diff-by-lrt"
#| echo: false
```
- The point-estimate line slopes upward, suggesting a larger Chelsea--Hackney gap at higher LRT values; because the interaction test is not statistically significant, treat this as descriptive rather than confirmed evidence of different slopes
- At the left end of the LRT range (low prior achievement), the difference between schools is relatively small
- At the right end (high prior achievement), the estimated Chelsea advantage is larger
- The confidence interval widens at extreme LRT values where fewer students are observed
- If the confidence band includes zero at some LRT values, it means the school difference is not statistically significant for those students

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-school-diff-by-lrt"
```
- `plot_comparisons()` from the `marginaleffects` package computes the pairwise comparison between factor levels (Chelsea vs. Hackney) at each value of the continuous conditioning variable (LRT)
- The dashed horizontal line at zero represents no school difference
- This plot directly visualizes @eq-ch5-school-diff: the school difference is a *linear function* of LRT, with slope $\beta_3$


:::


## Testing the Interaction {#sec-ch5-testing}

Finding a non-zero interaction coefficient in the output is not enough --- we need a formal statistical test to determine whether the interaction is significant. The most straightforward approach is to compare the two nested models using an F-test.

### Formal F-Test: Comparing Nested Models

The ANCOVA model (@eq-ch5-ancova) is *nested* within the interaction model (@eq-ch5-contcat). The ANCOVA model is a special case of the interaction model with the constraint $\beta_3 = 0$. We can test this constraint using the `anova()` function, which computes the F-test for comparing nested models.

The null and alternative hypotheses are:

$$
H_0: \beta_3 = 0 \quad \text{(parallel lines; no interaction)}
$$

$$
H_1: \beta_3 \neq 0 \quad \text{(non-parallel lines; interaction present)}
$$

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: ch5-interaction-test
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Formal F-test: does the interaction improve the model?
anova(model_parallel, model_interact)
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-interaction-test"
#| echo: false
```
- The `Res.Df` column shows the residual degrees of freedom for each model
- The `RSS` column shows the residual sum of squares --- a smaller value means better fit
- The `F` statistic tests whether the reduction in RSS achieved by adding the interaction term is large enough to be unlikely under the null hypothesis of parallel lines
- If $p < 0.05$, we reject the parallel lines assumption and conclude that the LRT slope differs significantly between schools

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-interaction-test"
```
- `anova(model1, model2)` performs a nested model comparison using an incremental F-test
- The first argument is the **restricted** (simpler) model: `model_parallel` with 3 parameters
- The second argument is the **full** (more complex) model: `model_interact` with 4 parameters
- The test has 1 numerator degree of freedom (one additional parameter: $\beta_3$)
- A significant p-value indicates that allowing different slopes significantly improves the model fit


:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Equivalence of Tests
For a single interaction term ($\beta_3$), three tests are equivalent:

1. The **F-test** from `anova(model_parallel, model_interact)`
2. The **t-test** for the `lrt:school_nameChelsea` coefficient in `summary(model_interact)`
3. The squared t-statistic equals the F-statistic: $t^2 = F$

When the interaction involves more than one coefficient (e.g., a continuous variable interacted with a 3-level factor producing two interaction terms), only the F-test provides a single omnibus test of the interaction.
:::

### When to Keep vs. Drop the Interaction

::: {.callout-tip}
## The "Test Interaction First" Principle
When building a model with potential interactions, follow this decision sequence:

1. **Fit the interaction model first** and test $H_0: \beta_3 = 0$
2. **If the interaction is significant** ($p < \alpha$):
    - Keep the interaction in the model
    - Interpret conditional effects (slopes and comparisons at specific values), not main effects
    - The main effects $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ are conditional on the other variable equaling zero, which may or may not be meaningful
3. **If the interaction is not significant** ($p \geq \alpha$):
    - A non-significant interaction often supports the simpler additive (ANCOVA) model --- especially in exploratory work --- so refit without the interaction, interpret the main effects from the simpler model, and note that the parallel-lines assumption is empirically supported
    - But do not treat the $p$-value as an automatic switch: **theory, study design, model hierarchy, statistical power, and interpretability** all bear on whether to keep an interaction the design was built to test. Conversely, a *significant* interaction can still be small or practically unimportant
:::

The logic behind this approach is that when an interaction is present, the main effect coefficients have a different (and often less useful) interpretation than when no interaction is present. Testing the interaction first determines *which interpretation applies* before we try to draw conclusions from the main effects.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Test the Interaction Before Interpreting It
**The principle.** When an interaction is *genuinely* present, reporting only the main effects can be seriously misleading. If the LRT $\times$ school interaction were real, then reporting that "Chelsea students score higher on average" without noting the interaction would obscure the fact that this difference depends on prior achievement: a student entering with a low LRT score would see a much smaller Chelsea advantage than the average suggests, while a student with a high LRT score would see a much larger one. In that case the interaction tells the complete story and the main effect alone tells a partial (and potentially misleading) one. This is exactly why a real interaction must not be ignored.

**What our data actually show.** In this GCSE example the interaction is *not* statistically significant ($F(1, 126) = 2.36$, $p = 0.13$). The point estimate $b_3 = 0.24$ hints that Chelsea's advantage grows with LRT, but the evidence is too weak to treat the slopes as genuinely different. The correct conclusion here is therefore to report the simpler **additive (ANCOVA) model** with a single, constant Chelsea--Hackney gap --- while having shown, above, how we *would* interpret the interaction if the test had been significant. The lesson generalizes: **run the formal test first**, and only switch from the additive interpretation to the conditional (interaction) interpretation when the test supports it.
:::

### Probing the Interaction: Conditional Slopes

Whether or not the interaction reaches significance, it is instructive to probe it by examining the effect of one variable at specific values of the other. (In this GCSE example the interaction is not significant, $p = 0.13$, so in practice we would report the simpler ANCOVA model; here we probe it for illustration.) For the continuous $\times$ categorical interaction, this means computing the LRT slope **separately for each school**.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: ch5-conditional-slopes
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Conditional slopes: LRT effect within each school
avg_slopes(model_interact, variables = "lrt", by = "school_name")
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-conditional-slopes"
#| echo: false
```
- The LRT slope for Hackney represents how much GCSE increases per unit increase in LRT for students in Hackney school
- The LRT slope for Chelsea represents the same quantity for Chelsea students
- If both slopes are positive but Chelsea's is larger, it means the fitted LRT–GCSE association is estimated to be steeper for Chelsea than for Hackney (note these data are observational and this slope difference is not statistically significant)
- The difference between the two slopes equals $\beta_3$, the interaction coefficient

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-conditional-slopes"
```
- `avg_slopes()` from the `marginaleffects` package computes the marginal effect (slope) of `lrt` separately within each level of `school_name`
- For the Hackney row: this is $\beta_1$ (the LRT slope when $d_{Ci} = 0$)
- For the Chelsea row: this is $\beta_1 + \beta_3$ (the LRT slope when $d_{Ci} = 1$)
- Each slope comes with its own standard error, confidence interval, and p-value


:::

### Model Comparison Summary

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: ch5-model-comparison
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Compare model fit statistics
compare_performance(model_parallel, model_interact, rank = TRUE)
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-model-comparison"
#| echo: false
```
- If the interaction model shows a substantially higher $R^2$ and lower AIC, the additional complexity of the interaction is justified
- If the improvement is trivial despite a significant p-value, the interaction may be statistically significant but practically unimportant
- BIC penalizes complexity more heavily than AIC and may favor the simpler model when the interaction effect is small

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-model-comparison"
```
- `compare_performance()` from the `easystats` ecosystem provides a side-by-side comparison of fit indices
- Key metrics include $R^2$ (variance explained), AIC, and BIC
- The `rank = TRUE` argument ranks models from best to worst on each criterion
- A meaningful improvement in $R^2$ combined with a significant F-test provides strong evidence for keeping the interaction


:::


## Categorical by Categorical Interaction {#sec-ch5-cat-cat}

When both predictors are categorical, the interaction tests whether the effect of one factor depends on the level of another factor. This is a common scenario in education research: does a gender gap vary across program types? Does the effect of an intervention differ by school level?

### The `faculty` Dataset

The `faculty` dataset from `regdatasets` contains salary data for 514 faculty members at a U.S. university. Key variables include `salary` (annual salary in dollars), `rank` (1 = Assistant, 2 = Associate, 3 = Full Professor), and `male` (1 = male, 0 = female). We will examine whether the gender salary gap depends on academic rank.

### The Mathematical Model

With gender (2 levels) and rank (3 levels, Assistant as reference), the interaction model requires:

- 1 dummy for gender ($d_M$ = Male)
- 2 dummies for rank ($d_{Assoc}$, $d_{Full}$)
- 2 interaction terms ($d_M \times d_{Assoc}$, $d_M \times d_{Full}$)

$$
y_i = \alpha + \beta_1 d_{Mi} + \beta_2 d_{Assoc,i} + \beta_3 d_{Full,i} + \beta_4 (d_{Mi} \times d_{Assoc,i}) + \beta_5 (d_{Mi} \times d_{Full,i}) + \epsilon_i
$$ {#eq-ch5-catcat}

### Interpreting the Cell Means

Each combination of gender and rank produces a distinct predicted mean. With Female and Assistant as references:

| Gender | Rank | E(Salary) |
|:-------|:-----|:----------|
| Female | Assistant | $\alpha$ |
| Male | Assistant | $\alpha + \beta_1$ |
| Female | Associate | $\alpha + \beta_2$ |
| Male | Associate | $\alpha + \beta_1 + \beta_2 + \beta_4$ |
| Female | Full | $\alpha + \beta_3$ |
| Male | Full | $\alpha + \beta_1 + \beta_3 + \beta_5$ |

The interaction coefficients have a specific meaning:

- $\beta_4$: How much the gender gap for Associate professors *differs* from the gender gap for Assistant professors
- $\beta_5$: How much the gender gap for Full professors *differs* from the gender gap for Assistant professors

If $\beta_4 = \beta_5 = 0$, the gender gap is the same at every rank --- the additive (no interaction) model is sufficient.

### Fitting the Model

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: ch5-cat-cat
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Faculty salary data: gender x rank interaction
data(faculty)
faculty_df <- faculty |>
  mutate(
    gender = factor(ifelse(male == 1, "Male", "Female")),
    rank_f = factor(rank, labels = c("Assistant", "Associate", "Full"))
  )

# Interaction model
model_cat <- lm(salary ~ gender * rank_f, data = faculty_df)
model_parameters(model_cat) |> print_md()
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cat-cat"
#| echo: false
```
- The intercept $\alpha$ is the mean salary for the reference cell (Female Assistant Professors)
- `genderMale` ($\beta_1$) is the gender gap among Assistant Professors specifically
- `rank_fAssociate` ($\beta_2$) is the Associate–Assistant gap among Females specifically
- The interaction terms tell us how the gender gap *changes* at higher ranks compared to the Assistant level

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cat-cat"
```
- `gender * rank_f` is shorthand for `gender + rank_f + gender:rank_f`, fitting main effects and their interaction
- R creates all pairwise products of the dummy variables automatically
- The interaction terms `genderMale:rank_fAssociate` and `genderMale:rank_fFull` test whether the Male–Female salary difference changes across ranks
- A general rule: with factor A ($a$ levels) and factor B ($b$ levels), the interaction requires $(a-1)(b-1)$ parameters


:::

### Visualizing the Interaction

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: fig-ch5-cat-cat
#| fig-cap: "Gender × Rank interaction plot of predicted salaries (points with 95% confidence intervals). The vertical male–female gap is not perfectly constant across ranks, but this apparent rank-dependence of the gender gap is not statistically significant (interaction F(2, 508) = 0.901, p = 0.41); see 'Testing the Overall Interaction'."
#| fig-alt: "Interaction plot with academic rank on the x-axis and predicted salary on the y-axis, showing separate points with 95 percent confidence intervals for male and female faculty at each rank (no connecting lines are drawn). The vertical gap between the male and female points changes across ranks, though the interaction is not statistically significant (p = 0.41). The gap appears largest at the full professor rank and smallest at the associate professor rank."
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

plot_predictions(model_cat, condition = c("rank_f", "gender")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(ber640_colors[["secondary"]], ber640_colors[["accent"]])) +
  labs(
    title = "Categorical × Categorical Interaction",
    subtitle = "Does the gender gap depend on rank?",
    x = "Academic Rank", y = "Predicted Salary",
    color = "Gender"
  )
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-cat-cat"
#| echo: false
```
- The plot shows predicted salary (y-axis) for each rank (x-axis), with separate points (and 95% confidence intervals) for Male and Female faculty
- If the gender gap (vertical distance) is roughly constant across ranks, the additive model is adequate and the interaction is weak
- If the gap widens or narrows at higher ranks, this visual non-parallelism suggests a meaningful interaction between gender and rank
- Each predicted mean is shown as a point, with its 95% confidence interval drawn as a vertical error bar

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-cat-cat"
```
- A male–female gap that changes across ranks (the vertical distance between the two points shifting from rank to rank) indicates a significant interaction
- A constant gap (the same vertical distance between the Male and Female points at every rank) would mean the gender gap does not depend on rank
- The vertical distance between the Male and Female points at each rank represents the gender gap *at that rank*


:::

An alternative and often more intuitive way to display categorical $\times$ categorical interactions is a grouped bar chart of predicted cell means with confidence intervals.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: fig-ch5-cat-cat-bars
#| fig-cap: "Grouped bar chart of predicted mean salary by gender and rank, with 95% confidence intervals. The height of each bar represents the model-predicted cell mean."
#| fig-alt: "Grouped bar chart with academic rank on the x-axis and predicted salary on the y-axis. Within each rank, two bars are shown side by side for male and female faculty, each with error bars representing 95 percent confidence intervals. The bars show that predicted salary increases from assistant to full professor for both genders, and the gender gap in bar heights varies across ranks."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Compute predicted cell means with 95% CIs
cell_means <- avg_predictions(model_cat, by = c("gender", "rank_f"))

ggplot(cell_means, aes(x = rank_f, y = estimate, fill = gender)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    width = 0.25, linewidth = 0.6
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(ber640_colors[["secondary"]], ber640_colors[["accent"]])) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted Mean Salary by Gender and Rank",
    subtitle = "Grouped bar chart with 95% confidence intervals",
    x = "Academic Rank",
    y = "Predicted Salary ($)",
    fill = "Gender"
  )
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-cat-cat-bars"
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
```
- Each pair of bars within a rank represents the predicted mean salary for Female and Male faculty at that rank
- The gender gap at each rank is the difference in bar heights between Male and Female
- If the gender gap (difference in bar heights) is roughly the same across all three ranks, the interaction is negligible
- If the gap clearly changes across ranks (e.g., small at Assistant but large at Full), this visual pattern reflects the interaction
- Overlapping confidence intervals within a rank suggest the gender difference at that rank may not be statistically significant
- This bar chart complements @fig-ch5-cat-cat by making the absolute salary levels (not just the line pattern) easier to compare

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-cat-cat-bars"
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
```
- `avg_predictions(model_cat, by = c("gender", "rank_f"))` computes predicted means for each of the six gender $\times$ rank cells, along with standard errors and 95% confidence intervals
- `geom_col(position = position_dodge())` places bars for Male and Female side by side within each rank
- `geom_errorbar()` overlays 95% confidence intervals on each bar, showing the precision of each cell mean estimate
- The visual comparison of bar heights within each rank shows the gender gap at that rank; comparing these gaps across ranks reveals the interaction


:::

### Testing the Overall Interaction

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: ch5-cat-cat-test
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Compare additive vs interaction model
model_cat_add <- lm(salary ~ gender + rank_f, data = faculty_df)
anova(model_cat_add, model_cat)
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cat-cat-test"
#| echo: false
```
- The `Res.Df` column shows residual degrees of freedom for the additive (larger) and interaction (smaller) models
- The `RSS` column shows how much unexplained variance remains in each model --- a lower value in the interaction model means the rank-specific gender gaps improve fit
- The $F$-statistic and $p$-value test whether the two interaction coefficients ($\beta_4$ and $\beta_5$) are jointly zero
- If $p < 0.05$, we conclude the gender gap is not constant across ranks and keep the interaction model
- If $p \geq 0.05$, the additive model is sufficient and we can interpret a single gender gap estimate across all ranks

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cat-cat-test"
```
- The $F$-test compares the additive model (parallel gender gaps) to the interaction model (rank-specific gender gaps)
- With 2 interaction parameters, this is an $F(2, n-6)$ test
- A significant $p$-value indicates the gender gap differs meaningfully across at least two ranks


:::

**What the test tells us here.** For these faculty data the interaction is **not statistically significant**: the two interaction coefficients are jointly indistinguishable from zero, $F(2, 508) = 0.901$, $p = 0.41$. In other words, although the predicted gender gap *appears* to differ across ranks in @fig-ch5-cat-cat and @fig-ch5-cat-cat-bars, we do **not** have evidence that the gender salary gap truly varies with rank --- the apparent pattern is well within what sampling noise would produce if the gap were constant. The honest conclusion is to report the simpler **additive model** (a single gender gap, the same at every rank). We still probe the interaction in @sec-ch5-probe-catcat, but purely to illustrate the conditional-comparison machinery, not as evidence that the gap is rank-dependent.


## Continuous by Continuous Interaction {#sec-ch5-cont-cont}

When both predictors are continuous, the interaction means the effect of one variable on $y$ changes as the other variable increases. This is the most challenging type of interaction to interpret because there is no natural grouping --- the moderating effect is continuous.

### The Mathematical Model

$$
y_i = \alpha + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \beta_2 x_{2i} + \beta_3 (x_{1i} \times x_{2i}) + \epsilon_i
$$ {#eq-ch5-contcont}

The critical insight is that the partial effect of $x_1$ on $y$ is no longer a constant --- it depends on the current value of $x_2$:

$$
\frac{\partial \text{E}(y)}{\partial x_1} = \beta_1 + \beta_3 x_2
$$ {#eq-ch5-contcont-slope}

Similarly, the partial effect of $x_2$ depends on $x_1$:

$$
\frac{\partial \text{E}(y)}{\partial x_2} = \beta_2 + \beta_3 x_1
$$

The interaction is **symmetric**: $x_1$ moderates the effect of $x_2$ and vice versa.

### Interpreting the Coefficients

In the interaction model, the "main effect" coefficients $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ take on a very specific (and often misunderstood) meaning:

| Coefficient | Interpretation |
|:------------|:---------------|
| $\alpha$ | E($y$) when $x_1 = 0$ and $x_2 = 0$ |
| $\beta_1$ | Effect of $x_1$ on $y$ **when $x_2 = 0$** |
| $\beta_2$ | Effect of $x_2$ on $y$ **when $x_1 = 0$** |
| $\beta_3$ | How much the effect of $x_1$ **changes per unit increase** in $x_2$ (and vice versa) |

::: {.callout-note}
## Centering Makes the Lower-Order Coefficients Interpretable
When $x_2 = 0$ is outside the range of the data, $\beta_1$ (the effect of $x_1$ "when $x_2 = 0$") describes an extrapolation and is hard to interpret on its own. **Centering** both predictors --- subtracting their means before forming the product --- fixes this: after centering, $\beta_1$ becomes the effect of $x_1$ *at the mean of* $x_2$, and $\beta_2$ the effect of $x_2$ *at the mean of* $x_1$. Importantly, **centering does not change the interaction coefficient $\beta_3$, the model's fit, or any prediction** --- it only relocates the zero point so the lower-order terms read off at a meaningful value. We fit the worked example below on the *raw* (uncentered) predictors so the coefficients map directly onto the raw-scale equation in @eq-ch5-contcont; Exercise 5.4 reruns the same model on centered predictors so you can see the lower-order coefficients become interpretable while $\beta_3$ stays identical.
:::

### Example: Education × Urbanization

We return to the crime data from @sec-ch4 to ask: does the protective effect of education on crime depend on how urbanized a county is?

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: ch5-cont-cont
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Crime data: education x urbanization interaction
crime_df <- crime |>
  rename(crime_rate = c, high_school = hs, urbanization = u)

model_cont <- lm(crime_rate ~ high_school * urbanization, data = crime_df)
model_parameters(model_cont) |> print_md()
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cont-cont"
#| echo: false
```
- The `high_school` coefficient is the effect of education **when urbanization = 0** --- meaningful here, since some counties are entirely rural (urbanization reaches 0 in these data)
- The `urbanization` coefficient is the effect of urbanization **when high_school = 0** --- but note that `high_school` never gets near 0 in these data (it ranges from about 54.5 to 84.9), so this coefficient is an **extrapolation outside the observed range** rather than a directly interpretable effect. Centering `high_school` would shift its zero to the sample mean and make this term interpretable (the interaction coefficient would be unchanged); see Exercise 5.4 for the centered version of this same model
- The interaction coefficient tells us the rate at which the education slope changes per percentage point increase in urbanization

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "ch5-cont-cont"
```
- `high_school * urbanization` fits three terms: `high_school`, `urbanization`, and their product `high_school:urbanization`
- The interaction term `high_school:urbanization` tests whether the slope of education on crime depends on the level of urbanization
- $\beta_3 > 0$ means: in more urban counties, the education effect is more positive (or less negative)
- $\beta_3 < 0$ means: in more urban counties, education is even more protective against crime


:::

**What the interaction term tells us here.** The estimated interaction coefficient is negative --- consistent with education being somewhat more protective in more urbanized counties --- but it is **not statistically significant** ($t \approx -0.97$, $p = 0.34$). We therefore do **not** have evidence that the education--crime slope genuinely changes with urbanization; the simpler additive model would be the one to report. As with the previous two examples, the conditional-slope, response-surface, and Johnson--Neyman displays that follow are used to teach *how to interpret and probe* a continuous $\times$ continuous interaction, not as confirmation that this particular interaction is real.

### Visualizing the Conditional Slope

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: fig-ch5-cont-cont
#| fig-cap: "How the effect of education on crime changes across levels of urbanization (continuous × continuous interaction). The slope and its 95% confidence band show where the education effect is statistically distinguishable from zero."
#| fig-alt: "Line plot with urbanization on the x-axis and the marginal effect of high school education on crime rate on the y-axis. A solid line shows the conditional slope of education, surrounded by a shaded 95 percent confidence band. At low urbanization the slope is near zero; as urbanization increases the point estimate of the education slope becomes more negative; however, the 95 percent confidence band includes zero at every urbanization level, so the education effect is not statistically significant anywhere in this range."
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

plot_slopes(model_cont,
  variables = "high_school",
  condition = "urbanization"
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title = "Continuous × Continuous Interaction",
    subtitle = "How the education slope changes with urbanization",
    x = "Urbanization (%)", y = "Slope of Education on Crime"
  )
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-cont-cont"
#| echo: false
```
- The line shows the estimated slope of high school education on crime rate at each level of urbanization
- In principle, where the confidence band lies entirely below zero increasing education would be significantly associated with lower crime --- but here the band includes zero across the whole urbanization range, so the education effect is non-significant at every level
- Where the band includes zero, the education-crime relationship is not statistically significant at that urbanization level
- The direction of the line's slope reveals whether education becomes more or less protective in urban versus rural areas
- This conditional slope plot is the key diagnostic for continuous $\times$ continuous interactions --- it translates an abstract product term into a substantive story about how one predictor's effect depends on another

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-cont-cont"
```
- `plot_slopes()` visualizes how the marginal effect (slope) of education changes as urbanization varies
- The confidence band shows where the education slope is significantly different from zero (band excludes the dashed line at zero)
- If the line is flat (horizontal), there is no interaction; a sloped line indicates the effect depends on the moderator
- The slope of this line equals $\beta_3$, the interaction coefficient


:::

### Response Surface: Heatmap of Predicted Values

Another powerful way to visualize a continuous $\times$ continuous interaction is a **response surface** --- a heatmap (or contour plot) showing predicted values of the outcome across the entire plane of the two predictors. Non-parallel contour lines indicate an interaction.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
```{r}
#| label: fig-ch5-response-surface
#| fig-cap: "Response surface heatmap showing predicted crime rate over the (high school education, urbanization) plane. The contour lines are slightly non-parallel, reflecting the estimated interaction term --- but that term is not statistically significant (t ≈ -0.97, p = 0.34), so the apparent twist is not strong evidence that the education-crime relationship changes with urbanization."
#| fig-alt: "Heatmap with high school education on the x-axis and urbanization on the y-axis, where color intensity represents predicted crime rate. Warmer colors indicate higher predicted crime. Contour lines overlay the surface and are slightly non-parallel, reflecting the estimated (but non-significant) interaction between education and urbanization. Crime is highest in areas with high urbanization and low education, and lowest where education is high and urbanization is low."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show R Code"

# Create a grid of predictor values
grid_df <- expand.grid(
  high_school = seq(
    min(crime_df$high_school), max(crime_df$high_school), length.out = 100
  ),
  urbanization = seq(
    min(crime_df$urbanization), max(crime_df$urbanization), length.out = 100
  )
)

# Compute predictions on the grid
grid_df$predicted <- predict(model_cont, newdata = grid_df)

# Heatmap with contour overlay
ggplot(grid_df, aes(x = high_school, y = urbanization, fill = predicted)) +
  geom_tile() +
  geom_contour(aes(z = predicted), color = "white", linewidth = 0.5, bins = 10) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = ber640_colors[["accent"]], mid = ber640_colors[["warning"]],
    high = ber640_colors[["secondary"]],
    midpoint = median(grid_df$predicted),
    name = "Predicted\nCrime Rate"
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = crime_df,
    aes(x = high_school, y = urbanization),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "white", size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Response Surface: Predicted Crime Rate",
    subtitle = "Slightly non-parallel contours reflect the estimated (non-significant) interaction; p = 0.34",
    x = "High School Education (%)",
    y = "Urbanization (%)"
  )
```

### Output Interpretation

```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-response-surface"
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
```
- The heatmap shows predicted crime rates across the full range of education (x-axis) and urbanization (y-axis)
- Warm gold-to-rust fills indicate higher predicted crime rates; muted slate/olive fills indicate lower predicted crime rates
- The **contour lines** are the key diagnostic: if they are parallel, the effect of education is the same at all levels of urbanization (no interaction); if they are not parallel, the interaction is present
- The direction of the contour tilt reveals the nature of the interaction: for example, contours that fan out in one direction indicate that the education effect strengthens (or weakens) with urbanization
- The white data points show the actual observations, revealing any gaps in the predictor space where predictions are extrapolations
- This visualization complements the conditional slope plot (@fig-ch5-cont-cont) by showing the full predicted surface rather than just the slope at one variable

### Code Explanation
```{r}
#| ref.label: "fig-ch5-response-surface"
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
```
- `expand.grid()` creates a dense $100 \times 100$ grid spanning the observed range of both predictors
- `predict()` computes the model-predicted crime rate at each grid point using the fitted interaction model
- `geom_tile()` draws the heatmap, with color representing the predicted crime rate
- `geom_contour()` overlays white iso-lines (contour lines of equal predicted crime rate)
- `scale_fill_gradient2()` uses a diverging color scale centered at the median predicted value
- `geom_point()` overlays the actual data points in white to show the observed predictor combinations
- In an additive (no interaction) model, the contour lines would be perfectly parallel; non-parallel contours reveal the interaction


:::

### Interactive Exploration: Continuous × Continuous Surface {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try It: Twist the Prediction Surface

Start by dragging the **β₃ (interaction X₁×X₂) slider** to see how the prediction surface changes from a flat plane (no interaction) to a twisted surface (interaction present). When β₃ = 0, the effect of X₁ is the same at every level of X₂. Then use the **β₁ and β₂ sliders** to set the two main effects, and switch the **View radio** (Surface / Slopes vs X₁ / Slopes vs X₂) to see the twist as either a heatmap or a fan of non-parallel conditional slopes; **Reset** returns every control to its starting value. We simulate the surface from a known equation rather than fitting real data because demonstrating how β₃ alone twists the plane requires counterfactual control over each coefficient — holding the truth fixed while you vary one term at a time — which no single observed dataset can give you.
:::

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
import { ber640 as contsurf_ber640, plotDefaults as contsurf_plotDefaults, themeColors as contsurf_themeColors, onThemeChange as contsurf_onThemeChange, seq as contsurf_seq, formatCoef as contsurf_formatCoef, withAlpha as contsurf_withAlpha, srSummary as contsurf_srSummary, resetControls as contsurf_resetControls } from "../ojs/shared.js"
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
contsurf_theme = contsurf_themeColors()
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
contsurf_pill = (label, value, tone = "") => html`<span class="ojs-readout ${tone ? "ojs-readout-" + tone : ""}"><span class="ojs-readout-label">${label}</span><span class="ojs-readout-value">${value}</span></span>`
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof contsurf_beta1 = Inputs.range([-2, 2], {label: "β₁ (effect of X₁)", step: 0.1, value: 1.0})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof contsurf_beta2 = Inputs.range([-2, 2], {label: "β₂ (effect of X₂)", step: 0.1, value: 0.5})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof contsurf_beta3 = Inputs.range([-1, 1], {label: "β₃ (interaction X₁×X₂)", step: 0.05, value: 0})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof contsurf_view = Inputs.radio(["Surface", "Slopes vs X₁", "Slopes vs X₂"], {label: "View", value: "Surface"})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

contsurf_intercept = 5

contsurf_predict = function(x1, x2) {
  return contsurf_intercept + contsurf_beta1 * x1 + contsurf_beta2 * x2 + contsurf_beta3 * x1 * x2;
}

contsurf_gridEdges = contsurf_seq(-3, 3, 31)

contsurf_surfaceData = {
  const data = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < contsurf_gridEdges.length - 1; i++) {
    const x1_min = contsurf_gridEdges[i];
    const x1_max = contsurf_gridEdges[i + 1];
    const x1 = (x1_min + x1_max) / 2;
    for (let j = 0; j < contsurf_gridEdges.length - 1; j++) {
      const x2_min = contsurf_gridEdges[j];
      const x2_max = contsurf_gridEdges[j + 1];
      const x2 = (x2_min + x2_max) / 2;
      data.push({x1, x2, x1_min, x1_max, x2_min, x2_max, yhat: contsurf_predict(x1, x2)});
    }
  }
  return data;
}

contsurf_yRange = {
  const vals = contsurf_surfaceData.map(d => d.yhat);
  return [d3.min(vals), d3.max(vals)];
}

contsurf_hasInteraction = Math.abs(contsurf_beta3) > 0.001

contsurf_equationStr = {
  const b1s = contsurf_beta1 >= 0 ? `+ ${contsurf_beta1.toFixed(1)}` : `− ${Math.abs(contsurf_beta1).toFixed(1)}`;
  const b2s = contsurf_beta2 >= 0 ? `+ ${contsurf_beta2.toFixed(1)}` : `− ${Math.abs(contsurf_beta2).toFixed(1)}`;
  const b3s = contsurf_beta3 >= 0 ? `+ ${contsurf_beta3.toFixed(2)}` : `− ${Math.abs(contsurf_beta3).toFixed(2)}`;
  return `Ŷ = 5 ${b1s}·X₁ ${b2s}·X₂ ${b3s}·X₁·X₂`;
}

contsurf_condColors = [contsurf_ber640.primary, contsurf_ber640.accent, contsurf_ber640.secondary]
contsurf_condLabels = ["-2", "0", "+2"]
contsurf_condVals = [-2, 0, 2]
contsurf_condDashes = ["6,4", "none", "2,3"]

contsurf_slopeX1Data = {
  const data = [];
  const xs = contsurf_seq(-3, 3, 60);
  for (let i = 0; i < contsurf_condVals.length; i++) {
    const x2 = contsurf_condVals[i];
    for (const x1 of xs) {
      data.push({x: x1, yhat: contsurf_predict(x1, x2), group: `X₂ = ${contsurf_condLabels[i]}`});
    }
  }
  return data;
}

contsurf_slopeX2Data = {
  const data = [];
  const xs = contsurf_seq(-3, 3, 60);
  for (let i = 0; i < contsurf_condVals.length; i++) {
    const x1 = contsurf_condVals[i];
    for (const x2 of xs) {
      data.push({x: x2, yhat: contsurf_predict(x1, x2), group: `X₁ = ${contsurf_condLabels[i]}`});
    }
  }
  return data;
}

contsurf_condYRange = {
  const allY = [...contsurf_slopeX1Data.map(d => d.yhat), ...contsurf_slopeX2Data.map(d => d.yhat)];
  return [d3.min(allY), d3.max(allY)];
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
contsurf_dashFor = (group) => {
  if (group.endsWith("-2")) return contsurf_condDashes[0];
  if (group.endsWith("+2")) return contsurf_condDashes[2];
  return contsurf_condDashes[1];
}

contsurf_endLabels = (data) => {
  const out = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < contsurf_condVals.length; i++) {
    const pts = data.filter(d => d.group.endsWith(contsurf_condLabels[i]));
    const last = pts[pts.length - 1];
    if (last) out.push({x: last.x, yhat: last.yhat, group: last.group});
  }
  return out;
}

contsurf_surfaceStops = [
  "#4EA3C8",
  "#D8D2C2",
  "#D97852"
]

contsurf_surfaceColor = {
  const [lo, hi] = contsurf_yRange;
  const mid = (lo + hi) / 2;
  const lowRamp = d3.interpolateRgb(contsurf_surfaceStops[0], contsurf_surfaceStops[1]);
  const highRamp = d3.interpolateRgb(contsurf_surfaceStops[1], contsurf_surfaceStops[2]);
  return function(y) {
    if (hi === lo) return contsurf_surfaceStops[1];
    if (y <= mid) return lowRamp((y - lo) / Math.max(mid - lo, 1e-9));
    return highRamp((y - mid) / Math.max(hi - mid, 1e-9));
  };
}

contsurf_surfaceLegend = {
  const [lo, hi] = contsurf_yRange;
  const mid = (lo + hi) / 2;
  return html`<div style="width:min(600px,100%);margin:6px auto 0;color:var(--ojs-text, ${contsurf_theme.text});font-size:0.78rem;line-height:1.25;">
    <div style="display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:center;margin-bottom:3px;font-weight:600;">
      <span>${contsurf_formatCoef(lo, 1)}</span>
      <span>Predicted Ŷ</span>
      <span>${contsurf_formatCoef(hi, 1)}</span>
    </div>
    <div aria-hidden="true" style="height:12px;border:1px solid var(--ojs-border, ${contsurf_theme.muted});border-radius:3px;background:linear-gradient(90deg, ${contsurf_surfaceStops[0]} 0%, ${contsurf_surfaceStops[1]} 50%, ${contsurf_surfaceStops[2]} 100%);"></div>
    <div style="display:flex;justify-content:space-between;margin-top:3px;color:var(--ojs-text-muted, ${contsurf_theme.muted});">
      <span>Lower</span><span>Midpoint ${contsurf_formatCoef(mid, 1)}</span><span>Higher</span>
    </div>
  </div>`;
}

contsurf_slopePlot = (data, xLabel) => Plot.plot(contsurf_plotDefaults({
  width: 600, height: 340,
  marginTop: 24, marginBottom: 40, marginLeft: 55, marginRight: 80,
  style: { color: "var(--ojs-text, " + contsurf_theme.text + ")" },
  x: {label: xLabel, domain: [-3, 3.6], grid: true},
  y: {label: "Ŷ", domain: contsurf_condYRange, grid: true},
  color: {
    domain: data === contsurf_slopeX1Data ? [`X₂ = -2`, `X₂ = 0`, `X₂ = +2`] : [`X₁ = -2`, `X₁ = 0`, `X₁ = +2`],
    range: contsurf_condColors,
    legend: false
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([contsurf_intercept], {stroke: "var(--ojs-text-muted, " + contsurf_theme.muted + ")", strokeDasharray: "3,3"}),
    Plot.line(data, {x: "x", y: "yhat", z: "group", stroke: "group", strokeWidth: 2.5,
      strokeDasharray: d => contsurf_dashFor(d.group)}),
    Plot.text(contsurf_endLabels(data), {x: "x", y: "yhat", text: "group", fill: "group",
      textAnchor: "start", dx: 6, fontWeight: 600, fontSize: 11}),
```
